tests.title: IELTS Academic Reading — Test 06
tests.description: Original full-length Academic Reading test: 3 passages, 40 questions, 60 minutes.

sections.title: Passage 1 — The Life of the Market Square
sections.instructions: You should spend about 20 minutes on Questions 1–13.
sections.passage:
The Life of the Market Square

¶1 For most of recorded history, the market square stood at the heart of the town. It was the place where farmers brought their produce, where craftsmen sold their wares, and where, almost incidentally, the life of the community was conducted. People came to buy and sell, but they stayed to exchange news, settle disputes and simply to see and be seen. The square was, in a sense, the town's living room, an open space that belonged to no one in particular and therefore to everyone. Long before the age of newspapers and screens, it was where a community learned what was happening to itself. To stand in the square on a busy morning was, in effect, to take the pulse of the whole town in a single glance.

¶2 The commercial logic of the market was always plain. By gathering many sellers in one place on a fixed day, the market spared buyers the trouble of searching from door to door, and it let them compare prices and quality at a glance. Sellers, for their part, reached far more customers than they could alone. This simple efficiency, drawing scattered supply and demand together at a single point, explains why markets sprang up independently in almost every settled society, and why the right to hold one was for centuries a privilege that towns guarded jealously and rulers granted as a favour. A single market day could transform a sleepy settlement, filling its streets with strangers and its inns with trade.

¶3 Yet the square was never merely an economic device. Because so many people passed through it, the market became the natural stage for everything a community did in public. Proclamations were read there, festivals were held there, and punishments were carried out there in full view. The constant, unplanned mixing of people of every rank, the wealthy merchant beside the poorest labourer, gave the square a social value quite separate from trade. It was one of the few places where the different parts of a town were obliged to encounter one another face to face. For a few hours each week, the ordinary barriers of rank and occupation were quietly suspended, and the town briefly saw itself whole.

¶4 The twentieth century was unkind to this ancient institution. As car ownership spread, the open square was often the most convenient place to park, and many were quietly surrendered to the motorcar. At the same time the supermarket and, later, the shopping centre offered shelter from the weather, longer opening hours and a far wider range of goods under a single roof. Against such competition the traditional market, open to the rain and trading only on certain days, began to look like a relic, and in town after town it dwindled or disappeared altogether. To many planners of the period the change seemed not a loss but the natural march of progress, and few mourned the passing of the draughty open stall.

¶5 Lately, however, the tide has begun to turn. Shoppers grown weary of the sameness of the supermarket have rediscovered the appeal of buying food directly from the person who grew or made it. Farmers' markets, once almost extinct, have multiplied, valued not only for the freshness of their produce but for the contact they restore between the eater and the source of the food. Town planners, meanwhile, have come to see a lively market as a way of drawing people back into centres that chain stores and online shopping had hollowed out.

¶6 What the new markets sell is, in part, an experience. A supermarket is designed for speed and efficiency, but a market is designed, however unconsciously, for lingering. The stallholder who can describe how a cheese was made, the chance encounter with a neighbour, the sheer sensory richness of colour, smell and noise, all offer something the most convenient website cannot. In an age when a great deal of shopping has become a solitary transaction conducted through a screen, the market provides the increasingly rare pleasure of buying as a social act. It is not, in truth, competing with the supermarket at all, but offering something the supermarket was never designed to provide.

¶7 None of this means the market square will ever again dominate a town as it once did; the supermarket and the smartphone are not about to vanish. But the persistence of the market, in defiance of every prediction of its death, suggests that it answers a need that pure efficiency cannot. People want, it seems, not only to acquire goods but to gather, to talk and to belong, and a town that keeps a place for them to do so is the richer for it. The market square, that oldest of urban inventions, may yet have a future as well as a past.

questions.text: The market square was historically used for social purposes as well as trade.
questions.explanation: Paragraph 1 says people came to buy and sell but 'stayed to exchange news, settle disputes', showing a social role beyond trade.

evidence.quote: they stayed to exchange news, settle disputes

questions.text: Markets developed in only a few unusual societies.
questions.explanation: Paragraph 2 says markets 'sprang up independently in almost every settled society', contradicting 'only a few'.

evidence.quote: sprang up independently in almost every settled society

questions.text: In the past, towns could hold a market whenever they wished, without permission.
questions.explanation: Paragraph 2 says the right to hold a market 'was for centuries a privilege' that rulers granted, so permission was needed.

evidence.quote: a privilege that towns guarded jealously and rulers granted as a favour

questions.text: The growth of car ownership harmed many market squares.
questions.explanation: Paragraph 4 says as car ownership spread, squares were 'quietly surrendered to the motorcar'.

evidence.quote: quietly surrendered to the motorcar

questions.text: Farmers' markets today attract more shoppers than supermarkets do.
questions.explanation: Paragraph 5 says farmers' markets have multiplied, but it never compares their shopper numbers with supermarkets, so this is Not Given.

questions.text: The text calls the square the town's living ______, open to everyone.
questions.explanation: Paragraph 1 describes the square as 'the town's living room'.

evidence.quote: the town's living room

questions.text: A market lets buyers compare ______ and quality at a glance.
questions.explanation: Paragraph 2 says the market let buyers 'compare prices and quality at a glance'.

evidence.quote: compare prices and quality at a glance

questions.text: Compared with a supermarket built for speed, a market is designed for ______.
questions.explanation: Paragraph 6 contrasts the supermarket's speed with a market 'designed, however unconsciously, for lingering'.

evidence.quote: a market is designed, however unconsciously, for lingering

questions.text: Which paragraph describes the square as a stage for public events such as festivals and punishments?
questions.explanation: Paragraph 3 says proclamations, festivals and punishments all took place there 'in full view'.

evidence.quote: punishments were carried out there in full view

questions.text: Which paragraph explains the basic economic efficiency of gathering sellers together?
questions.explanation: Paragraph 2 explains markets spared buyers searching door to door and drew 'scattered supply and demand together at a single point'.

evidence.quote: drawing scattered supply and demand together at a single point

questions.text: Which paragraph describes the decline of markets in the twentieth century?
questions.explanation: Paragraph 4 says the century 'was unkind to this ancient institution', describing how markets dwindled.

evidence.quote: The twentieth century was unkind to this ancient institution

questions.text: Which paragraph suggests markets meet a human need that efficiency alone cannot?
questions.explanation: Paragraph 7 says the market's persistence shows it 'answers a need that pure efficiency cannot'.

evidence.quote: it answers a need that pure efficiency cannot

questions.text: What single type of market has multiplied as shoppers seek food direct from its source?
questions.explanation: Paragraph 5 says 'Farmers' markets ... have multiplied', valued for restoring contact with the source of food.

evidence.quote: Farmers' markets, once almost extinct, have multiplied

sections.title: Passage 2 — Saving the Seed
sections.instructions: You should spend about 20 minutes on Questions 14–26.
sections.passage:
Saving the Seed

¶1 The food that feeds the world rests on a surprisingly narrow foundation. Of the many thousands of plant species that human beings have eaten over the millennia, a mere handful, wheat, rice, maize and a few others, now supply the great majority of the calories people consume. Within each of those crops, moreover, farmers increasingly plant the same small number of high-yielding varieties, bred for the conditions of recent decades. The result is a global food system of extraordinary productivity but also, many scientists warn, of dangerous uniformity. The very success of modern farming, in other words, has quietly narrowed the genetic base on which that success depends. History suggests that such narrowing, however efficient in the short term, rarely ends well.

¶2 Uniformity is a risk because a field of genetically identical plants is, in effect, a field with a single weakness. A new disease or pest, or simply a run of unusual weather, can sweep through such a crop with nothing to halt it, since every plant is vulnerable in exactly the same way. History offers grim warnings. When a fungus attacked the few potato varieties on which a whole population had come to depend, the failure of that single crop helped to trigger one of the worst famines of the nineteenth century. Diversity, by contrast, acts as a kind of insurance: in a varied population, some plants will almost always survive whatever the others cannot. A population is, in this sense, only as resilient as it is varied, and the lesson of past famines is that a crop without variety is a gamble with no fallback.

¶3 The raw material of that diversity is held in seeds, and for thousands of years it was preserved simply by farmers saving and replanting their own. Every season they kept back the seed of the plants that did best in their particular soil and climate, and over countless generations this produced a vast wealth of local varieties, each finely adapted to its corner of the world. As industrial agriculture spread and farmers turned to commercial seed bought afresh each year, this living library began to shrink, and many traditional varieties slipped quietly out of cultivation and towards extinction. Each variety that vanished took with it a unique set of adaptations that no laboratory could readily recreate. What farmers built up over ten thousand years, the modern world has contrived to erode in less than a century.

¶4 To guard against such losses, scientists began to establish seed banks: special stores in which seeds from as many varieties as possible are dried, sealed and kept at low temperatures, where they can remain alive for decades or longer. The most famous of these, built into a frozen mountainside on a remote Arctic island, was designed as a backup of last resort, a secure copy of the world's crop diversity that could survive even a regional catastrophe. Should a national collection be destroyed by war, fire or simple neglect, its varieties might still be recovered from this distant vault. The vault holds no living plants, only their dormant seeds, waiting in the cold for a day that may never come.

¶5 Yet a seed bank, for all its value, has an important limitation. A seed kept frozen in storage is frozen also in time: it cannot adapt, while out in the fields the pests, diseases and climate it must one day face continue to change. For this reason many researchers argue that storage alone is not enough, and that varieties must also be kept growing in real conditions, where they can continue to evolve. This approach, sometimes called conservation through use, treats farmers themselves as partners in preservation, keeping old varieties alive in the very environments to which they must remain suited. A variety that keeps facing real pests and real droughts, the argument runs, is a variety that keeps learning to survive them.

¶6 The stored diversity is not merely a museum of the past; it is the essential toolkit for the future. When breeders need to develop a crop that can tolerate drought, resist a new disease or thrive in saltier soil, they search these collections for varieties that already carry the necessary traits, often humble landraces long ignored by commercial agriculture. A single wild or traditional variety, worthless by the measures of the modern market, may hold exactly the genetic key that allows a staple crop to survive a changing climate. The seeds saved today, in other words, are the options available tomorrow. To lose a variety now is therefore to foreclose a solution that future farmers might one day desperately require.

¶7 The challenge is partly one of urgency and partly one of justice. Varieties are being lost faster than they can be collected and studied, and once a variety is gone, the unique combination of traits it carried is gone for good. There is also a longstanding argument about who owns this diversity, much of which was developed over centuries by farmers in poorer countries yet is now stored, studied and sometimes patented elsewhere. How fairly the benefits of the world's seeds are shared may, in the end, prove as important as how securely they are stored. A seed, after all, is at once a biological object and a piece of shared human heritage. To treat it as private property alone is to misunderstand what a seed truly is, and how it came to exist at all.

diversity developed in poorer countries is sometimes patented elsewhere
seed banks are running out of space

diversity developed in poorer countries is sometimes patented elsewhere

sections.title: Passage 3 — The Trouble with Forecasts
sections.instructions: You should spend about 20 minutes on Questions 27–40.
sections.passage:
The Trouble with Forecasts

¶1 Human beings have always wanted to know the future, and modern societies spend enormous sums trying to predict it. Economists forecast the growth of nations, meteorologists the path of storms, and analysts the behaviour of markets and populations. These predictions guide decisions worth billions, yet their record is decidedly mixed. Some forecasts are remarkably accurate; others fail so completely that they would have been bettered by a coin toss. Understanding why some futures can be foreseen and others cannot is among the more useful, and more humbling, lessons of modern science. It is a question that matters far beyond the seminar room, since societies routinely stake fortunes, and sometimes lives, on getting the answer right. For every forecast that quietly succeeds, another fails loudly enough to make the headlines.

¶2 A forecast is only ever as good as the model on which it rests, and a model is by definition a simplification. To make sense of a tangled reality, the forecaster must strip away most of its detail and keep only the few factors believed to matter most. Every model, in this sense, is a deliberate act of leaving things out. This is both the source of a model's power and the seed of its failures. A map that showed every pebble would be useless, but a map that omits a crucial bridge will lead the traveller astray. The art of modelling lies in knowing what may safely be ignored, and that judgement is far harder than it sounds. Leave out too little and the model drowns in noise; leave out too much and it quietly distorts the very thing it was built to capture.

¶3 The crucial distinction is between systems that are merely complicated and those that are genuinely complex. A complicated system, such as the orbit of the planets, may involve many parts, but those parts obey stable rules, so that astronomers can predict an eclipse centuries in advance with astonishing precision. A complex system, such as an economy or a climate, is different in kind. Its countless parts interact and influence one another, small causes can produce disproportionate effects, and the system can shift suddenly into a wholly new state. In such systems, long-range prediction is not merely difficult but may be fundamentally impossible. No increase in computing power can rescue a prediction whose subject refuses, in principle, to stay still. The distinction is not a matter of degree but of kind, and forgetting it is the source of many a confident forecast gone wrong.

¶4 Weather forecasting illustrates both the progress and the limits. Thanks to better data and faster computers, a modern five-day forecast is as reliable as a one-day forecast was a generation ago, a genuine triumph. Yet beyond about two weeks the atmosphere becomes unpredictable in principle, because tiny, unmeasurable differences in starting conditions grow, over time, into entirely different outcomes. This sensitivity, popularly summed up in the image of a butterfly's wingbeat eventually affecting a distant storm, sets a hard ceiling on how far ahead the weather can ever be known, however powerful our computers become. Some doors, the example suggests, are closed not by ignorance but by the very nature of the world itself. The achievement is real, but so is the boundary that no amount of effort can push back.

¶5 Faced with such limits, the wisest forecasters have changed what they try to do. Rather than offering a single confident prediction, they increasingly present a range of possible outcomes, each with a probability attached. Professor Helena Vasquez argues that this shift, from prediction to the honest mapping of uncertainty, is the most important advance in the field, since a forecast that hides its own doubt is worse than useless: it invites a false confidence that can prove disastrous when the unexpected arrives. A good forecast, on this view, does not tell you what will happen; it tells you how uncertain the future is. That is a less satisfying message than a confident prophecy, but it is a far more honest one.

¶6 This poses a problem for those who must act on forecasts. Politicians and the public alike tend to want firm answers, and a probabilistic forecast, hedged with ranges and caveats, is easily mistaken for evasion or incompetence. Dr Marcus Lindqvist observes that when a cautious, well-calibrated forecast competes for attention with a bold and simple one, the bold prediction usually wins the audience, even though it is more likely to be wrong. The forecasters whom the public trusts most, he notes with some irony, are frequently those who deserve that trust the least, precisely because they promise a certainty that the evidence cannot support. The incentives, in short, reward exactly the overconfidence that good forecasting is meant to avoid. The marketplace of predictions, left to itself, tends to select for boldness rather than for accuracy.

¶7 None of this means that forecasting is futile. Even an imperfect forecast, honestly presented, is far better than no forecast at all, and knowing the limits of prediction is itself a form of knowledge. A society that understands which futures can be foreseen, and which cannot, will plan differently and more wisely: building in flexibility where the future is genuinely uncertain, rather than betting everything on a single prediction that may not come to pass. The real failure is not the forecast that turns out to be wrong, but the false belief that the future can always be known in advance. Humility, in the end, may be the forecaster's most valuable instrument. To know what cannot be known is, paradoxically, one of the most practical kinds of wisdom a society can possess. A forecast worth trusting is, above all, one that is honest about the reach of its own vision.

questions.text: The writer says that all modern forecasts are highly accurate.
questions.explanation: Paragraph 1 says their record is 'decidedly mixed' and some fail completely, contradicting 'all highly accurate'.

evidence.quote: their record is decidedly mixed

questions.text: A model must leave out most of the detail of reality.
questions.explanation: Paragraph 2 says a model 'is by definition a simplification' and the forecaster must 'strip away most of its detail'.

evidence.quote: strip away most of its detail

questions.text: The orbit of the planets is given as an example of a genuinely complex system.
questions.explanation: Paragraph 3 uses planetary orbits as an example of a merely 'complicated' system, not a complex one.

evidence.quote: A complicated system, such as the orbit of the planets

questions.text: Weather forecasting has improved over recent generations.
questions.explanation: Paragraph 4 says a modern five-day forecast is now 'as reliable as a one-day forecast was a generation ago, a genuine triumph'.

evidence.quote: as reliable as a one-day forecast was a generation ago

questions.text: Most governments now require forecasts to include probabilities by law.
questions.explanation: The passage praises probabilistic forecasting but says nothing about legal requirements, so this is Not Given.

questions.text: The writer believes knowing the limits of prediction is itself valuable.
questions.explanation: Paragraph 7 states that 'knowing the limits of prediction is itself a form of knowledge'.

evidence.quote: knowing the limits of prediction is itself a form of knowledge

questions.text: According to the passage, the difficulty of modelling lies above all in
options: knowing what can safely be ignored
correct_answer: knowing what can safely be ignored
questions.explanation: Paragraph 2 says the art of modelling 'lies in knowing what may safely be ignored'.

evidence.quote: knowing what may safely be ignored

questions.text: What makes a complex system different from a complicated one?
options: its parts interact so that small causes can have large effects
correct_answer: its parts interact so that small causes can have large effects

evidence.quote: small causes can produce disproportionate effects

options: tiny differences in starting conditions grow into different outcomes

evidence.quote: tiny, unmeasurable differences in starting conditions grow

questions.text: A good forecast, the writer suggests, mainly tells you

evidence.quote: it tells you how uncertain the future is

questions.text: The shift from single predictions to mapping uncertainty is the field's most important advance.
questions.explanation: Paragraph 5: Vasquez argues this shift 'is the most important advance in the field'.

evidence.quote: is the most important advance in the field

questions.text: When a cautious forecast competes with a bold one, the bold prediction usually wins the audience.
questions.explanation: Paragraph 6: Lindqvist observes that 'the bold prediction usually wins the audience, even though it is more likely to be wrong'.

questions.text: Which sensitivity to starting conditions is popularly summed up by the image of a butterfly's ______?
questions.explanation: Paragraph 4 refers to 'the image of a butterfly's wingbeat eventually affecting a distant storm'.

evidence.quote: the image of a butterfly's wingbeat

questions.text: Where the future is genuinely uncertain, the writer says a wise society should build in ______ rather than bet on one prediction.
questions.explanation: Paragraph 7 says a wise society plans 'building in flexibility where the future is genuinely uncertain'.

evidence.quote: building in flexibility where the future is genuinely uncertain
